title: Urban Wi-Fi Gridlock Predicted To Arrive in 2004 (Kastner/Aberdeen report)
author: eWEEK staff
abstract: eWEEK coverage of Peter Kastner (Aberdeen) report 'The Urban Wi-Fi Crash of 2004' predicting urban Wi-Fi interference gridlock. Kastner (senior analyst at Boston-based Aberdeen) argues that more powerful/advanced 802.11 flavors won't help users because they are automatically 'dumbed down' by other access points. Notes purchases shifting to 802.11g for throughput but without solving the interference problem. Framed as threatening hot-spot users in urban shadows of overlapping access points.
importance_rationale: Rare named Aberdeen report forecasting a Wi-Fi-specific interference crisis, one of the first public analyst calls anticipating the overcrowded-2.4GHz problem that eventually drove 5GHz migration and mesh/smart-AP adoption.
relevance_rationale: Interference/co-channel issues remain central to Wi-Fi design debates in 2026 (2.4GHz regulation, 6GHz Wi-Fi 6E/7, CBRS). Kastner's framing presaged the need for spectrum-management innovation.
prescience_rationale: Core interference thesis validated — urban 2.4GHz spectrum did become congested by 2007-2010. 'Dumbed-down' framing anticipated Wi-Fi rate-adaptation. But the predicted 'crash of 2004' was averted by rapid 802.11n/5GHz adoption and power-management/CSMA refinements — partial prescience rather than full vindication.
